text: This video demonstrates the use of a homemade, inexpensive and easily constructed hand port to facilitate laparoscopic segmental sigmoid colon resection. The patient is a fifty nine year old male with a body mass index of thirty two kilograms per meter squared. Eight weeks prior he underwent a combined endolaparoscopic resection of a ten millimeter sessile sigmoid colon polyp. Pathology showed the polyp to contain a two millimeter poorly differentiated invasive cancer with clear resection margins. The patient consented to proceed with segmental sigmoid colon resection to stage and treat potential nodal involvement.

Upon insertion of the laparoscope, the surgeon observes the sigmoid mesentery, noting the challenge posed by the fatty and short small bowel mesentery. The patient is positioned in steep Trendelenburg with the left side elevated, but this results in minimal displacement of the small bowel. A tattoo marking the polypectomy site is visible on the distant colon.

The surgeon attempts to improve exposure of the sigmoid mesentery by positioning the omentum over the liver and flipping small bowel loops kephalad, but large appendices epiploicae on the sigmoid colon hinder the view.

The surgeon performs lateral mobilization of the sigmoid colon after abandoning the medial to lateral dissection due to poor exposure. The left ureter is identified and protected during the procedure.
The surgeon identifies the distal level of planned bowel transection at the pelvic brim. A window is created in the mesentery adjacent to the bowel, and the bowel is divided at this level.

The surgeon constructs a homemade hand port using a wound retractor and a glove to improve exposure of the sigmoid mesentery. A small incision is made, and the wound retractor is inserted. The surgeon places a glove over his existing glove on the right hand, stretches it over the retractor ring, and tightens the retractor onto the abdomen. Pneumoperitoneum is re-insufflated to maintain abdominal cavity pressure.

With hand assistance, the surgeon exposes, dissects, and divides the sigmoidal branches amidst overlying small bowel loops. The sigmoid colon is exteriorized and transected through the fan steel incision. The anvil of a 31mm circular stapler is sewn in with a proline purse-string suture, followed by re-insufflation of pneumoperitoneum and creation of a stapled end-to-end colorectal anastomosis.
An air leak test is performed, confirming an airtight colorectal anastomosis.
